coredata.dc:title: Hydrangea-like MoS2/carbon dots anode for high-performance sodium storage
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yan
Wang, Qing
Liu, Wuxin
Wei, Yuli
Wang, Shasha
Luo, Shaohua
Zhang, Yahui
Hou, Pengqing
Yan, Shengxue
Liu, Xin
Guo, Jing
coredata.dc:description: Owing to their quantum size, edge effects, and abundant surface functional groups, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant attention. In this study, chitin-derived carbon dots (CT-CDs) were prepared and used to synthesize MoS2/CT-CDs. The abundant functional groups on the surface of the CT-CDs facilitated the orderly arrangement of MoS2 nanosheets, resulting in a hydrangea-like structure. When employed as the anode in a sodium-ion battery, MoS2/CT-CDs exhibited an excellent initial charging capacity of 492.6 mAh·g−1 at 0.1 A·g−1 with an initial Coulomb efficiency of 74.4%. Even after 100 cycles, the reversible capacity remained at 338.9 mAh·g−1. Furthermore, the reversible capacity of MoS2/CT-CDs remained at 219.9 mAh·g−1 after 260 cycles when subjected to 1 A·g−1. The hydrangea-like structure of MoS2/CT-CDs, with expanded layer spacing, enhances ion/electron transport while providing additional active sites for sodium-ion storage, resulting in exceptional cycling and rate performances.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
MoS2
Anodes
Sodium-ion battery

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52104291, No.52274295), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (Nos. E2021501007, E2021501029, E2022501029 and E2022501028), the Natural Science Foundation-Steel, the Iron Foundation of Hebei Province (No. E2022501030), the Science and Technology Project of Hebei Education Department (ZD2022158), the Central Guided Local Science and Technology Development Fund Project of Hebei Province(226Z4401G), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N2023040, N2123035, N2223010 and N2223009), Performance subsidy fund for Key Laboratory of Dielectric and Electrolyte Functional Material Hebei Province (No. 22567627H).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of MoS2/CT-CDs.
(a) XRD pattern of MoS2/CT-CDs and MoS2; (b) FTIR spectrum of CT-CDs and MoS2/CT-CDs; (c, d) Raman spectra of MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs, respectively.
XPS spectrum of MoS2/CT-CDs. (a) XPS full spectrum; (b, c, d, e) high-resolution spectra of Mo3d, S2p, C1s and N1s of MoS2/CT-CDs.

The SEM and TEM images of MoS2/CT-CDs. (a, b) SEM maps at different rates; (c, d) TEM maps at different resolutions; (e, f) HRTEM maps at different resolutions; (g) STEM maps; (h, i, j, k) Element Mapping.
BET patterns and corresponding pore size distribution curves. (a, c) N2 adsorption isotherms of MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs; (b, d) Pore distribution curves of MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs.

Electrochemical Na storage performance. (a) CV curves of MoS2/CT-CDs; (b) initial charge/discharge curves of MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs; (c) MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs rate properties; (d) 0.1 A·g−1 cycle properties of MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs; (e) long cycle performance of MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CD at 1.0 A·g−1.

Kinetic analysis of Na storage for MoS2/CT-CDs anode. (a) CV curves at various sweep rates; (b) logarithmic fit between peak current and sweep rate; (c) area occupied by capacitance contribution at 1 mV·s−1; (d) contribution of pseudo-capacitance at various sweep rate.
Nyquist plots of pure MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs. (a, b) Nyquist plots of pure MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs before and after long cycling.

head.ce:title: Hydrangea-like MoS2/carbon dots anode for high-performance sodium storage

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to their quantum size, edge effects, and abundant surface functional groups, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant attention. In this study, chitin-derived carbon dots (CT-CDs) were prepared and used to synthesize MoS2/CT-CDs. The abundant functional groups on the surface of the CT-CDs facilitated the orderly arrangement of MoS2 nanosheets, resulting in a hydrangea-like structure. When employed as the anode in a sodium-ion battery, MoS2/CT-CDs exhibited an excellent initial charging capacity of 492.6 mAh·g−1 at 0.1 A·g−1 with an initial Coulomb efficiency of 74.4%. Even after 100 cycles, the reversible capacity remained at 338.9 mAh·g−1. Furthermore, the reversible capacity of MoS2/CT-CDs remained at 219.9 mAh·g−1 after 260 cycles when subjected to 1 A·g−1. The hydrangea-like structure of MoS2/CT-CDs, with expanded layer spacing, enhances ion/electron transport while providing additional active sites for sodium-ion storage, resulting in exceptional cycling and rate performances.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have gradually become inadequate for meeting the demands of the energy storage market, leading to an increased focus on new energy storage systems[1–4]. Na+ and Li+ belong to the same main group in the periodic table and exhibit similar physical and chemical properties. Furthermore, the abundance of reserves and similar energy storage mechanisms make sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) a promising candidate for replacing LIBs [5–9]. However, the relatively large ionic radius and atomic mass of Na+ are not favorable for rapid migration in electrode materials [10–12]. Therefore, the development of suitable anodes is crucial for the successful application of SIBs [13].

Metal sulfides offer high theoretical specific capacities and energy densities, making them ideal anodes for SIBs. MoS2 possesses a graphene-like layered structure with a weak van der Waals interlayer force, facilitating rapid channels for the intercalation/deintercalation of Na+ [14]. The theoretical Na storage capacity of MoS2 can reach up to 600 mAh·g−1 [15]. However, the poor intrinsic conductivity of MoS2 hinders the transmission efficiency of ions and electrons. Moreover, MoS2 undergoes volumetric expansion during charging and discharging, resulting in the deformation of electrode materials[16]. Furthermore, considering the intercalation-conversion Na storage mechanism of MoS2 anodes, intercalation and conversion reactions require short diffusion paths and high specific surface areas, respectively. Effective strategies such as nanosizing, morphology control, and compositing with carbon materials have been employed to enhance the Na storage properties of MoS2 [17–19]. Carbon materials exhibit high conductivity and can achieve rapid charge transfer and reduce agglomeration. MoS2 with different morphologies, such as nanosheets [20], microspheres[21], and nanotubes[22], can shorten the Na+ transport distance and alleviate volume expansion. For example, an anode comprising MoS2/graphene composite paper for SIBs delivered a capacity of 338 mAh·g−1 at 25 mA·g−1 [23]. Moreover, hierarchical MoS2@RGO nanosheet composite anodes exhibited an excellent reversible capacity of 420 mAh·g−1 after 160 cycles at 0.1 A·g−1 [24].

Owing to their quantum size, edge effects, and abundant surface functional groups, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant attention [25]. Compared with multidimensional carbon materials, CDs offer superior dispersibility and ultra-small sizes, making them suitable connectors and inducers for constructing nanostructures with various morphologies. Furthermore, the increased surface area of CDs leads to more interfaces between the electrodes and the electrolyte, thereby enhancing the electrochemical activity of the electrode material. For example, CDs have been introduced as “design additives” in the synthesis of TiO2 to form C-TiO2 composites. As the CD content increases, the morphology of C-TiO2 can transition from nanoparticles to nanoneedles and even 3D petal-like structures[26]. In addition, CDs at different concentrations have been employed to prepare CDs/NiCo2O4 nanocomposites using a hydrothermal method[27]. Owing to the influence of CDs, the CDs/NiCo2O4 composites exhibited sea urchin-like, flower-like, and bayberry-like shapes. The specific capacitance of the optimal sample at 1 A·g−1 was 2168F·g−1, surpassing that of pure NiCo2O4. These results show that CDs can play a pivotal role in regulating the morphology of composite materials and enhancing the electrochemical performance of composite electrodes.

In this study, chitin-derived CDs (CT-CDs) were prepared using chitosan as the carbon source via a hydrothermal method. The CT-CDs were employed as functional structural inducers to facilitate the assembly of MoS2 nanosheets into MoS2/CT-CDs nanocomposites with hydrangea-like structures. Compared with the initial block-like structure of MoS2, the hydrangea-like structure aids in shortening the diffusion path of Na+, thereby mitigating the volumetric expansion that occurs during charging/discharging, and enhancing the cycling stability and rate performance of the anodes.

Chitin and ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate ((NH4)6Mo7O24·4H₂O) were acquired from Aladdin Biotechnology Co. Hydrochloric acid (HCl) was obtained from Tianjin Komeo Chemical Reagent Co. Thiourea (CH4N2S) was purchased from Weifang Xingtai Chemical Co. All of these chemicals were of analytical-grade and required no further purification

In this study, CT-CDs were prepared via a hydrothermal method using chitin as the source. The chitin was initially soaked in dilute HCl for 12 h, then washed to neutrality and dried at 80 ℃. Next, 3 g of the obtained chitin powder and 50 mL of deionized water were added to a sealed 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and then heated to 180 ℃ at a rate of 5 ℃·min−1 for 6 h. The resulting product was centrifuged to collect the supernatant, which was filtered. The filtrate was dialyzed for 48 h in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da. The dialyzed solution was refrigerated for 24 h and then placed in a freeze-dryer for 48 h to obtain CT-CDs.

(NH4)6Mo7O24·4H₂O was used as the molybdenum source and CH4N2S as the sulfur source. 0.5 m mols (0.62 g) of NH4)6Mo7O24·4H₂O, 15 m mols (1.17 g) of CH4N2S, 20 mg of CT-CDs, and 30 mL of deionized water were added into 100 mL of sealed Teflon-lined autoclave and heated for 20 h at 200 °C with a heating rate of 5 ℃·min−1. Subsequently, the product was repeatedly washed and dried to obtain the MoS2/CT-CDs. The experimental process is illustrated in Fig. 1 . For comparison, pure MoS2 without CT-CDs was prepared using the same method.

The final samples were characterized via X-ray diffraction (XRD, Smartlab) using Cu-Ka radiation with a wavelength (λ) of 0.15406 nm and a scanning angle (2θ) between 10 and 80°. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SUPRA-55) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI-Tecnai G2 F20) were employed to observe the microstructure of the final products. A Fourier-transform infrared spectrophotometer (FT-IR-8400 s) was used to analyze the surface functional groups. Raman spectroscopy (HORIBA Scientific Laboratory, RAM HR Evolution) was used to study the phases of the samples. Elemental analysis was conducted using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). A pore size and specific surface area analyzer (Beijing Biotechnology Electronics Co., Ltd., SSA-4300) was employed to determine the pore structural parameters of the samples. The content of CT-CDs or MoS2 in the composites was determined through thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) with a heating rate of 10 °C·min−1.

A mixture comprising 80 % active material, 10 % acetylene black, and 10 % polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) was prepared in a mortar. An appropriate amount of NMP was added and continuously ground until a well-mixed slurry with a specific viscosity and flowability was achieved. The resulting slurry was evenly spread onto a clean copper foil collector. Subsequently, the Cu foil was compacted using a roller press and punched into a circular working electrode with a radius of 5 mm. After drying in a vacuum oven at 120 ℃ for 8 h, the loading mass of the active substance was approximately 1.3 mg·cm2. The electrolyte was a mixture of 1 M NaClO4 with dimethyl carbonate (DMC) and ethyl carbonate (EC) (1/1 vol%). A Whatman GF/D glass fiber was employed as the separator. CR2032 coin cells were assembled in an Ar-filled glove box. These assembled cells were used for all electrochemical tests. Constant-current charge and discharge performance tests were conducted using a Neware battery tester. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was analyzed using a CHI 1000 electrochemical workstation with a voltage range from 0.01 to 3.0 V. Electrochemical impedance tests were conducted using the same workstation at an amplitude of 10 mV and a frequency range of 100 kHz ∼ 0.01 Hz.

Microstructural images of the CT-CDs obtained via TEM are illustrated in Fig. S1a and b. The TEM images show that the CT-CDs exhibit sphere-like nanoparticles, approximately 10 nm in size. Fig. S1c shows the XRD spectra of the CT-CDs. At 2θ = 23°, a broad diffraction peak can be observed, indicating that the prepared CT-CDs have a low degree of graphitization. Fig. S1d shows the FTIR spectra of the CT-CDs, where the characteristic peaks of the stretching vibrations can be attributed to OH (3389 cm−1), CH (2927 cm−1), CO (1697 cm−1), CC (1635 cm−1), CN (1521 cm−1), and CO (1045 cm−1). The peaks between 2266 cm−1 and 2335 cm−1 correspond to the OCO absorption peak. The absorption peak at 1419 cm−1 represents the CH internal bending vibration. The FTIR spectra indicate that the CT-CDs were enriched in nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups.

XPS analysis was conducted to further confirm the surface chemical elemental composition of the CT-CDs. The XPS profiles (Fig. S2a) confirm the presence of C, O, and N in the CT-CDs. In the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. S2b), the four peaks appearing at 284.3, 285.5, 287.2, and 289.1 eV represent CC, CN, COH, and CO, respectively. In the high-resolution spectrum of N1s (Fig. S2c), the peaks at 398.6 and 399.1 eV represent NH and CN, respectively. Meanwhile, in the high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. S2d), the peaks at 530.3, 531.3, and 532.7 eV correspond to COC, CO, and COH, respectively. The results of XPS and FTIR analyses indicate that the surface of the CT-CDs contains abundant functional groups, which can form various surface defects and generate more active sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and analysis of MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The structural properties of pure MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs were analyzed using XRD, as shown in Fig. 2 a. The series of characteristic peaks at 2θ = 13.86°, 33.23°, and 59.03° for pure MoS2 can be attributed to the (002), (100), and (110) crystal planes of MoS2, respectively. The high-intensity (002) peak indicates that pure MoS2 exhibits a well-layered stacking structure. The layer spacing was calculated to be 0.636 nm using the Bragg equation[28]. In the XRD spectra of the MoS2/CT-CDs, apart from all the characteristic diffraction peaks of MoS2, no other peaks can be observed, indicating that the addition of CT-CDs did not cause a phase change in MoS2. Using the Bragg equation, the layer spacing in the (002) plane of MoS2/CT-CDs was determined to increase to 0.658 nm. The diffraction peaks of MoS2/CT-CDs are significantly broader than those of pure MoS2, which may be attributed to the presence of CT-CDs that inhibit the growth of MoS2 crystals[29].

The MoS2/CT-CDs were further characterized using FT-IR spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 2b. Both MoS2/CT-CDs and CT-CDs exhibit characteristic peaks at 2927 cm−1 (CH), absorption peaks at 1521 and 1419 cm−1 representing CN and CH3, respectively. In addition, the peaks between 2266 and 2335 cm−1 represent OCO. These results indicate that CT-CDs were also distributed on the surface of the MoS2/CT-CDs.

The Raman spectra of pure MoS2 and the MoS2/CT-CDs are shown in Fig. 2c and d, respectively. Three distinct peaks appear at approximately 358, 380, and 408 cm−1 in Fig. 2c, representing the E1 g, E1 2g, and A1 g vibration modes of laminar MoS2, respectively. The E1 2g and A1 g peaks are associated with the in-plane and out-of-plane vibrations of Mo and S atoms, respectively [30]. In Fig. 2d, the peaks at approximately 336, 375, and 404 cm−1 correspond to the E1 g, E1 2g, and A1 g vibration modes, respectively. Moreover, two characteristic bands appear at 1334 (d-band) and 1545 cm−1 (G-band), representing disordered and graphitic structures, respectively. Therefore, the Raman spectral analysis results indicate the coexistence of MoS2 and CT-CDs in the composite.

Furthermore, to determine the mass ratio of the CT-CDs in the composite MoS2/CT-CDs, the heat-loss curves of the composites were measured, as shown in Fig. S3. Only one phase of weight loss occurred throughout the process, which can be attributed to the oxidation of MoS2 to MoO3 and the decomposition of carbon in air. Calculations indicate that the CT-CD content in MoS2/CT-CDs was approximately 12.1 %.

The compositions of the surface elements and the chemical states of the MoS2/CT-CDs composites were explored using XPS, as illustrated in Fig. 3 . The full-scan XPS spectra (Fig. 3a) clearly show that the MoS2/CT-CDs mainly contained Mo, C, N, O, and S. The C and N elements were derived from the CT-CDs, further indicating that MoS2 was successfully compounded with CT-CDs. The high-resolution Mo spectra are shown in Fig. 3b. The peaks at approximately 228.9 and 232.1 eV correspond to Mo4+ 3d5/2 and Mo4+ 3d3/2, and the peaks at 229.6, 232.5, and 235.8 eV represent Mo5+3d5/2, Mo6+3d5/2 and Mo6+3d3/2, respectively, which may be caused by slight oxidation. The high-resolution spectrum of S (Fig. 3c) shows two characteristic peaks at 161.8 and 163.2 eV, which can be attributed to S 2p2/3 and S 2p1/2, respectively. In the C spectrum (Fig. 3d), the three peaks at 284.8, 286.6, and 288.2 eV correspond to CC, CO, and OC–O, respectively. In the high-resolution spectrum of N (Fig. 3e), the peaks at 398.1, 399.6, and 402.8 eV represent pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic N, respectively[31]. The findings of the XPS analysis agree with the FT-IR results, indicating that CT-CDs were also distributed on the surface, introducing more defects on the surface of MoS2/CT-CDs and generating more active sites.

The microstructures of MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs were investigated using SEM and TEM. The SEM images of pure MoS2 at various magnifications are shown in Fig. S4a and b. Nanosheet structures were observed on the surface, and bulk pure MoS2 was formed by the random stacking of these nanosheets. The TEM images of pure MoS2 (Fig. S4c, d) further confirm that bulk MoS2 was formed via irregular stacking of these nanosheets. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images in Fig. S4e and f indicate that the layer spacing in the (002) plane of MoS2 is 0.636 nm, which is consistent with the XRD results. Fig. S4g–i shows selected areas of the elemental analysis and scanning energy spectrum (SEM-EDS) images of pure MoS2. Uniformly distributed Mo and S can be observed, indicating that the bulk structure formed by the stacked nanosheets was MoS2.

Fig. 4 shows the SEM and TEM images of the MoS2/CT-CDs composites. In Fig. 4a and b, a hydrangea-like structure assembled by MoS2 nanosheets can be observed. The transition from the bulk structure of pure MoS2 to the hydrangea-like structure of MoS2/CT-CDs suggests that the presence of an appropriate amount of CT-CDs plays a significant role in promoting the formation of MoS2/CT-CDs. The microstructures of MoS2/CT-CDs were further characterized via TEM. As illustrated in Fig. 4c and d, compared with the random stacking of nanosheets in pure MoS2, the nanosheets at the edges of the MoS2/CT-CDs form regular “petals” of a hydrangea. Upon the incorporation of CT-CDs, the abundant functional groups on the surface of the CT-CDs provided nucleation sites for MoS2 and facilitated the regular reassembly of MoS2 nanosheets to form hydrangea-like structures. Fig. 4e and f show the HRTEM images of MoS2/CT-CDs, revealing that the layer spacing of MoS2/CT-CDs (0.658 nm) is larger than that of MoS2 (0.638 nm). The expansion of the MoS2/CT-CDs interlayer spacing can be attributed to the combination of CT-CDs and MoS2 during the hydrothermal process. The CT-CDs acted as structure-directing agents within the interlayers of MoS2 and connected with MoS2 nanoparticles through abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, effectively preventing stacking and agglomeration between the faces, thus maintaining a larger interlayer spacing[32]. The large layer spacing was conducive to the insertion/de-insertion of Na+ during the charging and discharging processes. Fig. 4g–k shows STEM and elemental mapping plots, respectively. The plots show the uniform distribution of the four elements, Mo, S, C, and N, which is consistent with the XPS results. The presence of C and N indicates the successful formation of MoS2/CT-CDs.

The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs were analyzed to further examine the pore structures. In Fig. 5 a and c, the isotherms of MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs exhibit type IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, indicating that the samples had a microporous/mesoporous/microporous layered structure[33]. The specific surface area of MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs is 1.606 and 3.715 m2·g−1, respectively. The pore distributions of the two samples are shown in Fig. 5b and d, and the inset shows the enlarged microporous and mesoporous regions. As shown in Fig. 5d, the pore sizes of MoS2/CT-CDs are primarily in the 2–26 nm range (mesopores), with a small number of micropores measuring approximately 2 nm.

To investigate the Na+ charge storage behaviors of the MoS2/CT-CDs anodes, their electrochemical properties were assessed using CV. Fig. 6 a displays the CV curves of the MoS2/CT-CDs anode with a voltage of 0.01–3 V and a scanning rate of 0.1 mV·s−1. The first discharge curve of MoS2/CT-CDs (Fig. 6a) exhibits three reduction peaks at 0.05, 0.73, and 1.14 V, respectively. The broad peaks near 1.14 and 0.73 V represent the insertion of Na+ into the MoS2 interlayer to form NaxMoS2 (MoS2 + x Na+ + x e- → Na x MoS2) and the formation of solid electrolyte interface (SEI) films. The peak near 0.05 V may be related to the conversion of NaxMoS2 to Na2S and metallic Mo (Na x MoS2 + (4-x)Na+ + (4-x)e- → Mo + 2Na2S)[34]. Furthermore, the two oxidation peaks at 0.54 and 1.64 V can be attributed to NaxMoS2, as well as the reconversion of metallic Mo and Na2S to MoS2, respectively [35]. In the following second and third cycles, the peaks of reduction/oxidation at 1.75 V and 1.5 ∼ 1.75 V are linked to the intercalation and deintercalation of Na+ in MoS2 and the interconversion reaction between Na2S and S, respectively[36]. The CV curves of the 2nd and 3rd cycles are nearly identical, indicating that the MoS2/CT-CDs electrode has high electrochemical reversibility and cycling stability.

Fig. 6b shows the initial charge–discharge curves of the MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs anodes at 0.1 A·g−1. Fig. 6b shows that the MoS2 anode exhibits an initial discharge capacity of 863.7 mAh·g−1 and a charge capacity of 450.3 mAh·g−1 with a low initial Coulombic efficiency (ICE) of 52.1 %. This capacity loss may be attributed to the formation of an SEI layer owing to irreversible reactions, as well as the decomposition of the electrolyte. After 100 cycles, the reversible capacity of the MoS2 anodes was reduced to 48.1 mAh·g−1. The primary reason for this decrease is that the pure MoS2 electrode undergoes severe volumetric expansion during cycling, causing the destruction of the electrode structure, which increases the diffusion distance for Na+ ions and ultimately results in rapid capacity decay. At the same current density, the MoS2/CT-CDs anodes delivered an initial discharge capacity of 662.4 mAh·g−1 and charge capacity of 492.6 mAh·g−1, with a high ICE of 74.4 %. After 100 cycles, the reversible capacity of MoS2/CT-CDs was 338.9 mAh·g−1. The outstanding cycling stability of the MoS2/CT-CDs anode suggests that the hydrangea-like structures and enlarged interlayer spacing facilitate the insertion of Na+ and alleviate volumetric expansion, thus improving cycling stability.

The rate performances of MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs anodes at different current densities are shown in Fig. 6c. The figure shows that the reversible capacities of the MoS2 anode are 379.4, 276.0, 204.8, 169.5, 132.3, and 80.6 mAh·g−1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2 and 5 A·g−1, respectively. Notably, when the current was abruptly reduced back to 0.1 A·g−1, the reversible capacity recovered to 322.5 mAh·g−1. The reversible capacities of MoS2/CT-CDs at the same current density were 480.3, 386.1, 352.3, 313.1, 274.5, and 213.9 mAh·g−1. As the current returned to 0.1 A·g−1, the anodes maintained a capacity of 411.9 mAh·g−1. These results demonstrate that the MoS2/CT-CDs anodes exhibit exceptional rate performance compared to pure MoS2 anodes.

The cycling performance of MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs at 1 A·g−1 was examined. Fig. 6e shows that after 260 cycles, the reversible capacities of MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs are 76.9 and 219.9 mAh·g−1, respectively. The MoS2/CT-CDs with hydrangea-like structures exhibited excellent long-cycle performance. SEM was used to observe the microstructure of the MoS2/CT-CDs anode after cycling (Fig. S5). The structure of MoS2/CT-CDs remained intact, and no apparent cracks were observed on the surface, indicating its good structural stability. Table S1 shows a performance comparison of MoS2-based anodes for SIBs between our work and other literature reports, indicating that MoS2/CT-CDs show strong competitiveness.

To understand the charge storage mechanism and kinetic properties of MoS2/CT-CDs anodes, the CV curves of MoS2/CT-CDs electrodes at the scan rates of 0.1 mV·s−1–1 V·s−1 are shown in Fig. 7 a. The energy storage process at the anodes can be divided into two parts: surface pseudo-capacitance behavior and diffusion behavior (intercalating Na storage)[37]. The capacity contribution of the anode can be determined using Equations (1) and (2) [38].
(1)
i = a
v
b
#
(2)
log ( i ) = l o g ( a ) + b l o g ( v ) #

Where i is the current, v is the scanning rate, and a is a constant. The value of b can be determined from the slopes of the following curves plotted using log (i) and log (v)[39]. When b approaches 0.5, it is primarily a diffusion-controlled process, whereas when b is approximately 1, the charge storage processes are dominated by surface pseudo-capacitance [40]. As shown in Fig. 7b, log (i)–log (v) curves are plotted according to the CV curves for different sweeping rates. The anodic and cathodic peaks demonstrate b values of 0.97 and 0.87, respectively, which are relatively close to 1, suggesting that the MoS2/CT-CDs anode is primarily dominated by the pseudo-capacitance process[41].

Furthermore, the contribution of the pseudo-capacitance mixing mechanism can be quantitatively analyzed using Equation (3) [15].
(3)
i
( V )
=
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2
#

Where k1v and k2v1/2 are constants associated with the pseudo-capacitance and diffusion control processes, respectively, and they can be calculated from the slope and intercept of the fitted line of i(V)/v 1/2-v 1/2 [42]. Fig. 7c shows the pseudo-capacitance and diffusion contributions at 1 mV·s−1, where the brown area indicates the pseudo-capacitance contribution and the rest of the white area represents diffuse contributions. The calculated results for the capacitive contribution and diffusion control at various scanning rates are shown in Fig. 7d. Notably, the pseudo-capacitance contribution increases from 68.9 % to 95.0 % as the scanning rate varies from 0.1 mV·s−1 to 1 mV·s−1. Compared to the diffusion-controlled behavior, the greater influence of pseudo-capacitive behavior can effectively enhance Na+ diffusion and electron transfer, resulting in MoS2/CT-CDs electrodes exhibiting superior rate performance.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed for both the pure MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs anodes, as shown in Fig. 8 . The Nyquist diagram shown in Fig. 8 consists of semicircles in the high-frequency region and oblique lines in the low-frequency region. The semicircular part represents the charge transfer impedance (Rct), and the linear part represents the diffusion impedance of Na+ (Warburg impedance)[43]. The inset shows the equivalent circuit diagram. The EIS spectra of the MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs anodes were fitted using ZView software, based on the equivalent circuit diagrams. As shown in the plots, the impedance values of Rct and Warburg impedance of the MoS2/CT-CDs anodes are smaller than those of the MoS2 anodes, indicating faster electron and Na+ transport in the MoS2/CT-CDs anodes.

Moreover, the Na+ diffusion coefficients of MoS2 and MoS2/CT-CDs after cycling were calculated from the EIS data (Fig. S6). The calculated Na+ diffusion coefficient of MoS2/CT-CDs was 3.24 × 10-17, which was larger than that of MoS2 (1.11 × 10-17), explaining the superior rate performance of MoS2/CT-CDs. This can be attributed to the hydrangea-like structure and enlarged layer spacing, which exposed more active sites and provided more pores in the MoS2/CT-CDs composites. These factors significantly shortened the diffusion path of Na+ and facilitated the infiltration of the electrolyte as well as the adsorption and transport of Na+. The incorporation of CT-CDs enhanced the electrical conductivity of molybdenum disulfide, which reduced the charge transfer and Warburg impedance.

In this study, CT-CDs were prepared using biomass chitin as the precursor. MoS2/CT-CDs were synthesized by combining CT-CDs, ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate, and thiourea using a one-step hydrothermal method. When MoS2/CT-CDs were employed as the anode for SIBs, they maintained a high reversible capacity of 338.9 mAh·g−1 after 100 cycles at 0.1 A·g−1. Even at a higher current density of 1 A·g−1, they maintained a reversible capacity of 219.9 mAh·g−1 after 260 cycles. The exceptional electrochemical properties of MoS2/CT-CDs can be attributed to two factors. First, in this study, CT-CDs were used as a structure-directing agent to modulate the microscopic morphology of MoS2 to form a hydrangea-like structure. The unique hydrangea-like structure and enlarged layer spacing provided more active sites and pore channels[15,19], facilitating the adsorption and transport of Na+ and significantly enhancing the kinetic performance of the electrochemical reaction. Second, MoS2/CT-CDs prepared via CT-CD induction exhibited lower impedance while suppressing electrode material failure caused by agglomeration and fragmentation, thus adapting to the volume change during battery operation and improving long-term cycling stability[23].

Yan Wang: Writing - original draft. Qing Wang: Writing - review & editing. Wuxin Liu: Data curation. Yuli Wei: Data curation. Shasha Wang: Data curation. Shaohua Luo: Resources. Yahui Zhang: Resources. Pengqing Hou: Resources. Shengxue Yan: Resources. Xin Liu: Resources. Jing Guo: Resources.
